name: Clare Fischer
description: American keyboardist, composer, arranger, and bandleader (1928–2012)
abstract: Douglas Clare Fischer (October 22, 1928 – January 26, 2012) was an American keyboardist, composer, arranger, and bandleader. After graduating from Michigan State University (from which, five decades later, he would receive an honorary doctorate), he became the pianist and arranger for the vocal group the Hi-Lo's in the late 1950s.

works.t: Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs: Someday My Prince Will Come

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Dobbins
about_quotes.evidence: one of my favorites, the biggest harmonic influence after Ellington and Strayhorn has been Clare Fischer.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bill Dobbins interviewed by Monk Rowe, Rochester, New York, January 11, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: I remember in New York Clare Fischer had flown out from L.A. He did these arrangements... That arrangement on In the Beginning is very special
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Vince Lateano
about_quotes.evidence: I joined Cal I guess around May of ’78. And Clare Fischer, and Poncho, Rob Fisher, myself and Cal... having played with those musicians.
about_quotes.doc_title: Vince Lateano interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Francisco, California, August 8, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: John Pizzarelli, Jr.
about_quotes.evidence: And there’s Clare Fischer, and there’s John Clayton, there’s Patrick Williams, there’s Johnny Mandel, there’s Michel Legrand and his entire French orchestra who just came in off a plane to play a concert... But the record gets made.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Pizzarelli, Jr. interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 29, 2000 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee only responds with 'Um-hm' to the interviewer's statements about Clare Fischer's work and usage by other artists, indicating awareness but no direct personal or professional interaction described.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Manny Albam
about_quotes.evidence: Clare Fisher, Bill Potts, who did that marvelous thing on “Porgy and Bess” some years ago, he brought all that music and played it...
about_quotes.doc_title: Manny Albam interviewed by Monk Rowe, Tarrytown, New York, October 31, 1998
